metadata.title: MLB Historical Salary Data for Comparison, 1920-1948
metadata.methodology_note: MLB salary data is better documented than Negro Leagues data but still has gaps in the pre-1950 era. Average salary figures before 1950 are estimates based on SABR research, economic histories, and the limited surviving records. Star salaries are better documented than averages.

sources.citation: SABR Business of Baseball Research Committee, 'MLB's Annual Salary Leaders Since 1874.'
SABR Business of Baseball, 'Ballplayer Pay and Performance in the Face of Economic Depression' (2009).
EH.net, 'The Economic History of Major League Baseball.'
Negro Leagues Baseball eMuseum, 'Life As a Black Baseball Player: Playing Conditions.' Gardner and Shortelle (1993).
Baseball Almanac, 'Major League Baseball Minimum Wage.'

era_averages.notes: Annual major league average $5,000-$6,000 per Gardner and Shortelle (1993), cited in NLBM eMuseum. MLB wages more than doubled during the 1920s while non-agricultural wages increased only 21% (SABR BoB 2009). EH.net reports 1920s average nominal salary of $6,992.

Annual major league average approximately $7,000 per Gardner and Shortelle (1993). EH.net reports 1930 average nominal salary of $7,748. MLB wages increased 27% during the 1930s while general wages gained only 8% (SABR BoB 2009).

From the 1940s through the late 1960s, the average major league salary was $6,000 (Baseball Almanac). After 1946 player negotiations, minimum salary set at $5,000. EH.net reports 1949 average nominal salary of $11,197. The late 1940s saw significant salary growth.

annual_estimates.notes: Ruth's salary per Baseball-Reference. Average estimated from era data.
The $7,200 figure used in the hook for average pitcher salary is derived from this range (season-length adjusted).
Ruth's $80,000 was 2.4x the second-highest salary (Rogers Hornsby). EH.net reports average of $7,748.
Depression impact. Slight salary decreases and purchasing power drops in 1933-1934 per SABR BoB.
Many top players serving in military.
Post-war salary jump. New $5,000 minimum established. EH.net reports mean of $18,500 (likely including higher-paid returnees).
Jackie Robinson earned $5,000 in his first MLB season.

salary_context.mlb_meal_money_notes: Cited in spec from Lester. Could not be independently verified via web search, but the disparity with Negro Leagues meal money ($1.00-$1.50/day) is consistent with broader salary gaps.
salary_context.mlb_1920s_wage_growth: MLB wages more than doubled during the 1920s. Non-agricultural wages increased only 21%.
salary_context.mlb_1930s_wage_growth: MLB wages increased 27% during the 1930s, almost all in the first two years. General wages gained only 8%.
salary_context.mlb_players_vs_general_population: Ballplayers fared much better than the general populace during the Depression. They did not experience double-digit unemployment and their salaries grew relative to the average American salary.
